user_summary: An empirically-oriented researcher at the intersection of traditional computer security and AI safety, focused on the full adversarial attack surface of large language models. Core interests run from automated jailbreak generation (GCG-style gradient-based suffix attacks, transferable attacks across proprietary models) to indirect prompt injection targeting LLM-integrated applications and tool-using agentic systems — the precise arc covered by the three seed papers. The researcher also tracks training-time attacks (data poisoning, backdoors), inference-time extraction (model stealing, training data leakage), and cryptographic defenses (watermarking, secure inference, verifiable computation about models). Their epistemic style is systems-and-measurement first: they want concrete attack demonstrations evaluated on real deployed models, adaptive evaluations that stress-test defenses, and work that travels between CCS/USENIX Security and NeurIPS/ICML. They explicitly follow frontier-facing institutional red-teaming — Anthropic's alignment science and red team, UK AISI, Apollo, and METR — and the CMU/ETH Zurich/CISPA cluster of academic ML-security researchers.
current_question: How robust are current frontier models to adaptive jailbreak attacks? What does security look like for agentic systems that use tools and browse the web?
interests: adaptive jailbreak attacks on frontier language models
indirect prompt injection in agentic systems
transferable adversarial attacks across language models
multimodal adversarial inputs to language models
model extraction and stealing attacks on production LLMs
training data poisoning and backdoor attacks
training data extraction and memorization from language models
agentic AI security for tool-using and web-browsing agents
frontier model red-teaming and capability evaluations
AI control and monitoring for potentially misaligned agents
watermarking and content provenance for language model outputs
cryptographic secure inference and verifiable computation for models
adaptive evaluation of adversarial defenses against language models

authors.name: Nicholas Carlini
authors.why: Seed paper co-author; leads adversarial attack research on LLMs at Anthropic — jailbreaks, training data extraction, model stealing — with a relentless focus on adaptive evaluation.

authors.name: Florian Tramèr
authors.why: Seed paper co-author; runs the SPY Lab covering LLM stealing, privacy attacks, and adversarial robustness from a cryptography-meets-security angle; created the AgentDojo agentic-security benchmark.

authors.name: Andy Zou
authors.why: Lead author on the GCG universal adversarial attack paper (seed 1); empirical bridge between alignment and adversarial-ML communities.

authors.name: Eric Wallace
authors.why: Pioneered universal adversarial triggers for NLP and adversarial prompt injection; key figure bridging adversarial NLP and LLM safety.

authors.name: Daniel Kang
authors.why: Studies LLM reliability and security under adversarial conditions including jailbreaks and deployment-time attacks on production systems.

authors.name: Aleksander Mądry
authors.why: Foundational adversarial robustness work (PGD attacks); shapes worst-case evaluation methodology that underpins all subsequent LLM attack research.

authors.name: Zico Kolter
authors.why: GCG paper co-author; works on certified defenses and adversarial robustness for neural networks.

authors.name: Sam Toyer
authors.why: Coined the 'prompt injection' framing and among the first to study adversarial security of LLM-integrated and agentic systems.

authors.name: Milad Nasr
authors.why: Seed papers 1 and 2 co-author; frequent Carlini collaborator on privacy attacks, training data extraction, and adversarially aligned models.

authors.name: Matt Fredrikson
authors.why: GCG paper co-author; initiated the study of model inversion attacks; core ML-security researcher at CMU CyLab.

authors.name: Sahar Abdelnabi
authors.why: First-author of the indirect prompt injection paper (seed 3); now leads her own group on LLM security; foundational work adopted by NIST, MITRE, and OWASP.

authors.name: Mario Fritz
authors.affiliation: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
authors.why: Co-authored the indirect prompt injection paper (seed 3); broad ML security and privacy portfolio at CISPA; heads the MLSEC research group.

authors.name: Matthew Jagielski
authors.why: Seed paper 2 co-author; specializes in data poisoning, training data extraction, and model security — spans both attack and defense sides.

authors.name: Christopher A. Choquette-Choo
authors.why: Seed paper 2 co-author; membership inference, differential privacy, and training data privacy attacks on large models.

authors.name: Pang Wei Koh
authors.why: Seed paper 2 co-author; influence functions and data poisoning foundations; connects statistical ML theory to concrete security threats.

sources.name: Anthropic Alignment Science Blog
sources.why: Active in 2026 with posts on constitutional classifiers for jailbreaks, many-shot jailbreaking, sabotage evaluations, AuditBench, and alignment faking — the highest-density source for Anthropic's internal red-team and safety research.

sources.name: UK AI Security Institute Research
sources.why: Active through May 2026; publishes boundary-point jailbreaking, frontier cyber capability evaluations, sandbagging detection, and pre-deployment red-team reports for GPT-5.5 and Claude variants — one of the most technically substantive government AI security labs.

sources.name: METR Blog
sources.why: Active in 2026 with frontier risk reports, external reviews of lab safety cases, and red-teaming of agentic monitoring systems; primary source for autonomous-AI risk evaluation methodology.

sources.name: Apollo Research Blog
sources.why: Active in 2026; publishes AI control evaluations and safety research; organizational transparency posts on security and science communication norms.

sources.name: Redwood Research Blog
sources.why: Active in 2026; originated the AI control research paradigm; publishes on monitoring malign LLM agents, alignment faking, research sabotage, and deployment-time misalignment — directly relevant to agentic-security interests.

sources.name: FAR.AI
sources.why: Active in 2026 with Berkeley ControlConf and Seoul Alignment Workshop; publishes on adversarial robustness scaling, persuasion risks, and red-teaming methodology — bridges academic security and frontier-model evaluation.

sources.name: Nicholas Carlini's Research Site
sources.why: Active; Carlini posts new work on LLM vulnerabilities, data poisoning, model stealing, and adversarial evaluation — a reliable first-stop for empirical attack research.

sources.name: Florian Tramèr / SPY Lab
sources.why: Active in 2026; personal site for the SPY Lab at ETH Zurich — covers production LLM stealing, privacy attacks, and hosts AgentDojo; Tramèr also organizes the Real World AI Security conference (June 2026).

sources.name: MLSEC Research Group
sources.why: Active into 2026 (new members January 2026); Mario Fritz and Thorsten Holz's group at CISPA publishing on LLM vulnerabilities, adversarial attacks on ML systems, and differential analysis of language model security.

sources.name: Anthropic Research Publications
sources.why: Active through May 2026; primary index for all Anthropic research output including frontier red-team reports, jailbreak defenses, and agentic safety work — complements the alignment-science blog with broader publication coverage.

filter_outs: Pure interpretability work without security applications
generic capability leaderboards unrelated to security
notes: All three seeds are foundational empirical attack papers — the GCG universal adversarial suffix attack, the adversarial alignment assessment of aligned LLMs, and the indirect prompt injection paper — forming a tight cluster around automated jailbreaking, transferability, and agentic/pipeline attack surfaces. The epistemic style is uniformly systems-and-measurement-first: concrete exploits on real deployed models, evaluated adaptively. For sources, I prioritized the four institutional labs the user named (Anthropic, AISI, Apollo, METR) — all confirmed active in 2026 — and added Redwood (AI control), FAR.AI (robustness/red-teaming), and two academic-PI sites (Carlini, Tramèr/SPY Lab) plus MLSEC (Fritz/Holz at CISPA), all verified active; Goodfire was excluded as purely interpretability-focused. Author list expands the original eight with seven additional coauthors directly from the seed papers (Nasr, Fredrikson, Abdelnabi, Fritz, Jagielski, Choquette-Choo, Koh), all central to the ML-security community this researcher tracks.
